activities.activity: Lord Of The Rings - Hobbiton Movie Set
activities.comments:
This shore excursion takes you to the landscape that served as the backdrop for Hollywood’s epic saga – the Lord of the Rings. Deep in the New Zealand countryside, you’ll find Hobbiton, the actual set where the movies where filmed. Enjoy a guided tour and take your photo in front of Bilbo Baggins’ Hobbit Hole. Soak up sweeping views across the Kaimai Mountains that helped create movie magic and are even more impressive in person. 

12:05 pm – 4:35 pm
No excursion selected yet

Follow in the footsteps of Hobbits as you explore the mountains and valleys that set the stage for "Lord of the Rings." Discover the rugged Rakaia Gorge and the fantastic Canterbury Plains. Walk past sparkling lakes and glistening blue rivers. Look up at the majestic mountains that helped create movie magic. You’ll soon understand why they chose one of the most amazing places on earth to create Middle earth.

activities.activity: Celebrity Discovery Collection: Oamaru And Moeraki Boulders
activities.comments: The town of Oamaru reveals its charms during visits to a penguin colony, mysterious giant spherical boulders, a charming Victorian neighborhood, and a futuristic museum with a 19th century twist.

activities.activity: Dusky Sound & Doubtful Sound & Milford Sound, New Zealand (At Sea)

activities.comments: Whether novice or pro, using an SLR or a smartphone, you're sure to snap postcard-perfect photos of Eden's bay area on this two-hour walking tour guided by a local, international-award-winning professional photographer. As you skirt the Twofold Bay area on foot, you'll learn about the history of Eden, the southernmost town in New South Wales, its whaling heritage, its townspeople, its vibrant fishing industry, and other points of interest. With your photographer-guide's advice on framing a shot, lighting, and more, you're certain to capture the best moments of your visit to Eden, and for sure take away tips and tricks that will enhance your future travel photos.

activities.activity: Opening Session: Gadgets and Gear You Gotta Have
activities.comments: There's more to life than helping users -- there's cool stuff that lets you work smarter and play harder. Cool gadgets, gear, and gizmos are the focus of this unique kick-off to Advisor Summit. Come and see the latest and greatest must-haves our speakers use and recommend in this fun evening. Discover innovative hardware, software, and services that can make a big difference in your business and lifestyle. And, if you have something cool, use the open-mike to share it with your colleagues. See what you can do to take advantage of exciting opportunities, power your ideas, save time, be more efficient, and best of all, have some fun. Find out what's new, neat, and even necessary at this ultimate show-and-tell!

activities.activity: GENERAL SESSION: Overview of Microsoft Office Access 2007 from a Developer Perspective
activities.comments: Access 2007 provides developers with the ability to build much richer databases much more quickly. In this session you'll discusses new functionality for building client databases, and covers Access's new designers, new coding tools, the ability to create custom task panes, collecting data through email, and customizing the ribbon UI. In addition, we show the out-of-the-box "tracking applications" that will ship with Access 2007, and show some of the tools used to build those apps and that will ship in the Access Developer Extensions.

activities.activity: Ribbon Extensibility in Microsoft Office Access 2007
activities.comments: Say goodbye to commandbars, and say hello to the Ribbon! The Ribbon is at the core of the new user experience introduced in Microsoft Office 2007. The Ribbon provides an innovative way to display and organize commands that improves productivity and usability for end users. The Ribbon exposes a rich and flexible XML-based extensibility model. This session provides an overview of the extensibility model for the Ribbon in Microsoft Office Access 2007. We will cover the new programming model, best practices, and how to incorporate the Ribbon design philosophy into your applications. We will also discuss backward compatibility for custom commandbars in Access 2007.

activities.activity: Delivering Better Access Solutions with FMS Tools and Techniques
activities.comments: FMS is the leading third party tool vendor for Access with their Total Access line of products. Luke Chung, president of FMS, will provide a light-hearted overview of how third party tools can help you be more productive, deliver better solutions, reduce your headaches, and make you look like a champion. Luke will focus on the challenges of Access application development, why FMS created their solutions for their own development needs, and how they’ve delivered solutions to the community-at-large including international customers. Even if you never buy an FMS product, you’re guaranteed to be entertained and learn about the challenges of Access development from one of the industry’s gurus who's been there since Access began. Bring your questions.

activities.activity: Use Microsoft Access for Client-Server Solutions
activities.comments: Learn when and how to use Microsoft Access in client-server architecture. You'll see how to take advantage of Access' rapid application development capabilities, while using a database server to improve performance. You'll discover how client-server differs from standard Microsoft Access techniques. You'll explore and take home two example client-server applications: an MDB application that displays customers, orders, and order details, and an ADP application that provides the same functionality so you can compare and contrast. Both show you how to provide maximum functionality while moving as little data as possible, and can be the foundation for your client-server projects.

activities.activity: Best Practices for Creating Better Microsoft Access Applications
activities.comments: Learn the Microsoft Access architecture and development approach used by FMS, including database design, management of temporary data, learning from mistakes, and post-delivery management. You'll explore how to separate the application from the data, design tables, fields, and indexes, deal with cross-references, optimize queries, work with forms and reports, handle coding challenges, work with ADO/DAO, and handle math inaccuracies. You'll review code clean up and automated delivery processes, and learn about automated tools from FMS to minimize problems and improve deliverables.

In this session, you'll learn how to work with forms. You'll learn how you can best take advantage of some important form and control events. We'll take a look at the built-in form filtering features and see how to best take advantage of the OpenArgs property. You'll see how and when it's beneficial to change a form's recordsource at runtime. Finally, we'll explore some power combo box and list box techniques, and how to best work with subforms. After this session, you'll be ready fully harness the power of Access forms. Take home a database containing all the examples covered in this session.

activities.activity: KEYNOTE: Build Collaborative Solutions with Microsoft Office 2007

activities.comments: Writing VBA code for Access applications can be long and tedious. Database applications tend to be complex and must appropriately manage data, the user interface, and users. You can dramatically shorten the time required to debug complex code by making the best use of VBA's debugging tools. In this session you'll explore a variety of Access debugging tools and techniques. You'll see practical examples of using breakpoints, execution options during a break, using the local and immediate windows, and creating and managing watches during execution. In addition, you'll learn about the call stack and conditional compilation. You'll learn many debugging tips, tricks and traps.

activities.activity: Use Microsoft SQL Server Express for Local and Offline Data
activities.comments: SQL Server Express is the lightweight version of SQL Server 2005 and takes the place of MSDE. It offers some intriguing capabilities, including support for XCopy and ClickOnce deployment and connecting via database file paths. In this session, you'll learn how to build database applications based on SQL Server Express and how to manage databases both at design-time and runtime. You'll explore how the Visual Studio 2005 project system handles database files, how to deploy the database to multiple users, what happens if you make a change to a table and more. You'll see how you can build applications with better support for offline scenarios and how to synchronize data between SQL Server 2005 and SQL Server Express.

Microsoft Access developers need to be able to use SQL effectively to create applications that run fast, reduce demands on the database, and deliver the data the application needs. In fact, if you want to get the best possible performance you need SQL. SQL updates outperform recordset processing by orders of magnitude .This session will show you how to "think in SQL," how to ensure that you get the best possible performance, and how to solve several common problems in data-driven Access applications.

activities.activity: SQL Server 2005 Basics for Access, VB and .NET Developers
activities.comments: These days it is necessary for Access, Visual Basic, and .NET programmers to know quite a bit about Microsoft SQL Server. In the whirlwind tour of the brand new SQL Server 2005, you’ll learn all of the basics of working with this powerful product. We’ll explore SQL Server Management Studio and what it has to offer, and learn how to build and analyze T-SQL queries within the Management Studio environment. You’ll learn about the database components and how to work with them. We’ll complete the session with a crash course on SQL Server 2005 security.

Show IT departments that you have the skill set necessary to build Access applications that will remain useful and low maintenance in the future. In this session, we'll discuss best practices for architecting a robust and maintainable solution. We'll include sample code, tips, and techniques for removing the need for central IT to be involved, and cover some tools offerings that can make building manageable databases easier.

activities.activity: Create Unbound Applications with Microsoft Access and Microsoft SQL Server
activities.comments: Microsoft Access can support many more users than you might expect. The secret is combining Access and Microsoft SQL Server. In this session, you'll learn how to move beyond simple linked-table applications. You'll learn to create an efficient Access front end to a SQL Server database, using an Access database (MDB) or an Access Data Project (ADP). You'll see how to move beyond simple linked-table applications. You'll learn techniques for fetching and caching data that will lighten the load on the network and minimize resources on SQL Server. You'll learn how to create a stored procedure-driven application, employ pass-through queries effectively, work with ADO recordsets, and handle multi-user concurrency conflicts. This session shows you how to minimize development time using Access as a client.

activities.activity: Use Access 2007 to Create Lotus Notes-like Applications and More
activities.comments: Access 2007 has evolved into a tool for power users. You can create entire applications without code. There are templates to get you started. Your data can live on a server and replicate with the rich client. Much of this functionality is similar to Domino Designer but with great reporting. Also, you will see how to pull Domino data into Access and create forms with wizards. If you work with users who don't have Domino Designer, maybe you should look at Access 2007.

activities.activity: Microsoft Access Query Tips and Tricks
activities.comments: Microsoft Access makes it easy to construct complex queries without needing to know SQL. But there are challenges that require going beyond the graphical tools and wizards. In this session, you'll learn several advanced but practical techniques for solving common Access query problems. You'll see how to resolve ambiguous outer joins, use union queries to merge, sort, and classify data from multiple tables, use correlated subqueries in output columns and criteria, correctly format names and addresses that include optional parts, and more.

activities.activity: 20 Microsoft Access Reporting Tips and Tricks
activities.comments: The reporting capabilities of Microsoft Access are powerful, but not always obvious. It's easy to use the report wizards to get up and running, but then what? Discover all that you can do with Access reports. In this session you'll dig deep into some of the less obvious features of Access reporting. You'll explore ways to customize printouts at the row level. You'll examine six methods of filtering and reporting. You'll learn how to create exactly the reports you want.

activities.activity: Microsoft Office Access 2007: A Rich Client for Windows SharePoint Services 3.0
activities.comments: Access 2007 works very well with Windows SharePoint Services 3.0 to enable applications that utilize the best of the rich client and the best of the collaboration server. In addition, administrators can use SharePoint to make their users' applications more manageable. This session discusses linking to SharePoint lists, support for all the SharePoint data types, including Access reports in the SharePoint list of views, and automatically installing Access front-end databases from SharePoint. In addition, we discuss building applications that leverage server-side workflows and combine Access components with custom server pages, and taking SharePoint applications offline.

activities.activity: ADVISOR ANSWERS: Technical Q&A - Closing Session

activities.comments: Some things can't be done using VBA, such as returning the current version of Windows, locating the Windows temporary folder, and creating a desktop shortcut or quick launch icon. The Windows API (application programming interface) opens a new level of programming, letting you add exciting and important new capabilities to your applications without ActiveX controls or other dependencies. This session introduces you to Windows API programming. You'll discover 15 essential application tasks that can only be done with the Windows API. You'll get all the examples as .BAS modules that are easily used in your Access or Visual Basic applications.

activities.activity: Access Data Analysis Tips and Techniques
activities.comments: Database applications typically receive and output data, but in between those two steps is the important task of data analysis. Microsoft Access has the power to do more than just store data. It can perform useful analysis, ranging from simple aggregations to complex statistical calculations. In this session you'll learn how to create and use the powerful functionality provided by Access and other data analysis tools.
